summary: The greater and lesser sacs of the peritoneal cavity communicate through the epiploic foramen (of Winslow).
The greater and lesser sacs communicate through the inguinal canal.
The greater and lesser sacs are completely separate with no communication.
The portal vein is formed behind the neck of the pancreas by the union of the superior mesenteric and splenic veins.
The portal vein is formed by the union of the hepatic artery and the splenic artery.
The portal vein is formed in front of the body of the pancreas.
Calot's triangle contains the cystic artery, and is bounded by the cystic duct, common hepatic duct, and liver.
Calot's triangle contains the hepatic artery proper and is bounded by the portal vein and bile duct.
Calot's triangle is located in the left upper quadrant near the spleen.
The caval opening in the diaphragm is at T8, the oesophageal hiatus at T10, and the aortic hiatus at T12.
The aortic hiatus is at T8, the caval opening at T10, and the oesophageal hiatus at T12.
All three diaphragmatic openings are at the same vertebral level of T10.
The lesser sac (omental bursa) lies behind the stomach and in front of the pancreas.
The lesser sac lies in front of the stomach and behind the liver.
The lesser sac is located in the pelvis behind the urinary bladder.
The portal vein carries nutrient-rich blood from the gastrointestinal tract to the liver for processing.
The portal vein carries oxygenated blood from the lungs to the liver.
The portal vein drains blood from the liver into the inferior vena cava.
The epiploic foramen is bounded anteriorly by the free edge of the lesser omentum containing the portal triad.
The epiploic foramen is bounded anteriorly by the greater omentum.
The epiploic foramen is bounded anteriorly by the transverse mesocolon.
